Lab 4: Topic Analysis

Mateo Robbins

Background: Latent Dirichlet Allocation

Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a generative probabilistic model that treats each document as a mixture of topics, and each topic as a mixture of words. Understanding the key variables helps interpret model outputs:

Symbol Name Role
α (alpha) Hyperparameter Dirichlet prior on document-topic distributions. Small α → each document focuses on few topics. Set by researcher.
β (beta) Hyperparameter Dirichlet prior on topic-word distributions. Small β → each topic uses a focused vocabulary. Set by researcher.
θ (theta) Document-topic distribution Probability distribution over topics for each document. P(topic | document). Latent — must be inferred.
φ (phi) Topic-word distribution Probability distribution over vocabulary for each topic. P(word | topic). Called beta in tidytext. Latent — must be inferred.
z Topic assignment The hidden topic label allocated to each individual word token. The bridge between θ and φ. Latent — must be inferred.
w Observed word The only variable you actually see. Everything else is inferred from patterns in w.

The Generative Assumptions

LDA imagines that documents were written as follows:

  1. For each topic t, draw a word distribution φ_t ~ Dirichlet(β)
  2. For each document m, draw a topic distribution θ_m ~ Dirichlet(α)
  3. For each word position in that document:
    • Draw a topic assignment z ~ Multinomial(θ_m)
    • Draw the word w ~ Multinomial(φ_z)

Inference runs this process in reverse: given the observed words w, recover the most probable z, θ, and φ.

The Dirichlet Distribution

The Dirichlet distribution is a distribution over distributions — it produces probability vectors that sum to 1, making it ideal for representing topic mixtures and word mixtures. The concentration parameter (α or β) controls sparsity:

  • Small value (< 1): samples are sparse — most probability sits on one or two categories. Realistic for most text corpora.
  • Large value (> 1): samples are spread evenly across all categories.
  • Value = 1: uniform — all mixtures equally likely.

Lab Setup

library(quanteda)
library(tm)
library(topicmodels)
library(topicdoc)   
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(reshape2)
library(tictoc)
library(LDAvis)
library(tsne)
library(servr)
library(LexisNexisTools)

Load the Data

pre_files <- list.files(
  path = here::here("Nexis", "AI_conservation"),   # update to your folder
  pattern = "\\.docx$",
  full.names = TRUE,
  recursive = TRUE,
  ignore.case = TRUE
)

pre_dat <- lnt_read(pre_files)

pre_meta_df       <- pre_dat@meta
pre_articles_df   <- pre_dat@articles
pre_paragraphs_df <- pre_dat@paragraphs

tbl <- tibble(
  date     = pre_meta_df$Date,
  headline = pre_meta_df$Headline,
  id       = pre_meta_df$ID,
  text     = pre_articles_df$Article,
  source   = pre_meta_df$Newspaper
)

## Build the Corpus

We use `corpus()` from {quanteda} to build the corpus from our data
frame.


``` r
corpus <- corpus(tbl, text_field = "text")

Tokenize and Clean

tokens() handles punctuation and number removal. We also build a custom stopword list that removes common journalistic filler words that carry no thematic signal. We can return to this section to add additional words once we see the results.

add_stops <- c(quanteda::stopwords("en", source = "smart"),
               "said", "says", "say", "told", "according", # Journalistic tokens
               "vol", "document", "fig", "pg", "usepackage", # unrelated tokens
               "system", "systems", "1", "=", "$", # common but uninformative tokens
               "ai", "conservation",# Search Terms removed because they all in theory talk about them
               "0") 

toks <- tokens(corpus,
              remove_punct = TRUE) |> 
  tokens_tolower()

toks1 <- tokens_select(toks, pattern=add_stops, selection="remove")

Build the Document-Feature Matrix

Topic modeling requires a document-feature matrix (DFM). We trim rare terms (appearing in fewer than 2 documents) and remove empty rows — LDA cannot process documents with no terms.

dfm1 <- dfm(toks1)
dfm2 <- dfm_trim(dfm1, min_docfreq = 2) |> dfm_subset(ntoken(dfm1) > 0)

# Remove empty rows — LDA will error on these
sel_idx <- slam::row_sums(dfm2) > 0
dfm <- dfm2[sel_idx, ]

Step 1: Initial Model (Theory-Driven k)

Great, now we are almost ready to run a model. We just have to come up with an initial value for k, the number of latent topics present in the data. How do we do this? Let’s say I think there may be political, economic and environmental articles. So I will tell the model to look for 3 topics by setting the k parameter = 3.

k <- 3

topicModel_k3 <- LDA(dfm,
                      k,
                      method = "Gibbs",
                      control = list(iter = 100,
                                     verbose = 25,
                                     seed = 321))
## K = 3; V = 12368; M = 96
## Sampling 100 iterations!
## Iteration 25 ...
## Iteration 50 ...
## Iteration 75 ...
## Iteration 100 ...
## Gibbs sampling completed!

Inspect the Posterior Distributions

posterior() extracts the two key output matrices:

  • theta (θ): document × topic matrix — P(topic | document)
  • beta/phi (φ): topic × term matrix — P(term | topic)
result <- posterior(topicModel_k3)

beta  <- result$terms
theta <- result$topics

cat("phi dimensions  (topics × vocab):", dim(beta), "\n")
## phi dimensions  (topics × vocab): 3 12368
cat("theta dimensions (docs × topics):", dim(theta), "\n")
## theta dimensions (docs × topics): 96 3
# Top 10 terms per topic
terms(topicModel_k3, 10)
##       Topic 1     Topic 2       Topic 3        
##  [1,] "data"      "energy"      "drug"         
##  [2,] "species"   "data"        "cells"        
##  [3,] "models"    "model"       "data"         
##  [4,] "learning"  "carbon"      "protein"      
##  [5,] "detection" "study"       "model"        
##  [6,] "model"     "framework"   "expression"   
##  [7,] "images"    "development" "gene"         
##  [8,] "animal"    "trust"       "sleep"        
##  [9,] "deep"      "sustainable" "supplementary"
## [10,] "research"  "analysis"    "cell"

Step 2: Selecting k with Coherence Scores

An alternative to theory-driven k selection is to fit models across a range of k values and evaluate them statistically. We use topicdoc::topic_coherence(), which measures whether the top words of each topic co-occur frequently in the corpus — a proxy for human interpretability. We can do this efficiently by running a sequence of models, each with a different value of k.

Higher coherence = more semantically meaningful topics.

tic()
# Fit models for a range of k values
k_values <- seq(2, 15, by = 1)

coherence_results <- map_dfr(k_values, function(k){
  m <- LDA(dfm, k,
           method = "Gibbs",
           control = list(iter = 300, verbose = 0, seed = 321))
  coh <- topic_coherence(m, dfm)
  tibble(k = k, coherence = coh)
})
toc()
## 64.033 sec elapsed
summary_coherence <- coherence_results %>%
  group_by(k) %>%
  summarize(mean_coherence = mean(coherence))


# Plot coherence vs k — look for the peak or elbow
ggplot(summary_coherence, aes(x = k, y = mean_coherence)) +
  geom_line(color = "navy") +
  geom_point(color = "navy", size = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = coherence_results$k[which.max(coherence_results$coherence)],
             linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Mean topic coherence by number of topics (k)",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = k with highest coherence",
       x = "Number of topics (k)",
       y = "Mean coherence score") +
  theme_minimal()

Interpreting the plot: Look for the peak coherence value, or an “elbow” where adding more topics stops improving coherence meaningfully. Also inspect the top terms at two or three candidate k values — statistical criteria and human interpretability do not always agree. Your final choice of k should be justified on both grounds.

Step 3: Fit the Selected Model

Based on the coherence plot (and domain knowledge of the corpus), we select a final k value and fit the final model.

k <-   11 # update this based on your coherence plot

topicModel_k_select <- LDA(dfm, k,
                           method = "Gibbs",
                           control = list(iter = 1000,
                                          verbose = 25,
                                          seed = 321))
## K = 11; V = 12368; M = 96
## Sampling 1000 iterations!
## Iteration 25 ...
## Iteration 50 ...
## Iteration 75 ...
## Iteration 100 ...
## Iteration 125 ...
## Iteration 150 ...
## Iteration 175 ...
## Iteration 200 ...
## Iteration 225 ...
## Iteration 250 ...
## Iteration 275 ...
## Iteration 300 ...
## Iteration 325 ...
## Iteration 350 ...
## Iteration 375 ...
## Iteration 400 ...
## Iteration 425 ...
## Iteration 450 ...
## Iteration 475 ...
## Iteration 500 ...
## Iteration 525 ...
## Iteration 550 ...
## Iteration 575 ...
## Iteration 600 ...
## Iteration 625 ...
## Iteration 650 ...
## Iteration 675 ...
## Iteration 700 ...
## Iteration 725 ...
## Iteration 750 ...
## Iteration 775 ...
## Iteration 800 ...
## Iteration 825 ...
## Iteration 850 ...
## Iteration 875 ...
## Iteration 900 ...
## Iteration 925 ...
## Iteration 950 ...
## Iteration 975 ...
## Iteration 1000 ...
## Gibbs sampling completed!
tmResult <- posterior(topicModel_k_select)
terms(topicModel_k_select, 10)
##       Topic 1         Topic 2      Topic 3      Topic 4     Topic 5      
##  [1,] "coral"         "ai-2"       "sleep"      "species"   "drug"       
##  [2,] "reefs"         "expression" "cells"      "detection" "model"      
##  [3,] "climate"       "gene"       "ais"        "images"    "absorption" 
##  [4,] "water"         "bacterial"  "cell"       "animal"    "based"      
##  [5,] "global"        "c-di-gmp"   "guidance"   "data"      "solubility" 
##  [6,] "india"         "protein"    "dynamics"   "wildlife"  "modeling"   
##  [7,] "sustainable"   "sequence"   "expression" "dataset"   "oral"       
##  [8,] "environmental" "luxs"       "gene"       "learning"  "formulation"
##  [9,] "world"         "species"    "neurons"    "camera"    "ph"         
## [10,] "change"        "activity"   "chromosome" "models"    "models"     
##       Topic 6       Topic 7        Topic 8        Topic 9         Topic 10     
##  [1,] "carbon"      "data"         "energy"       "protein"       "data"       
##  [2,] "ecosystem"   "trust"        "solutions"    "mm"            "research"   
##  [3,] "development" "security"     "efficiency"   "data"          "technology" 
##  [4,] "landscape"   "blockchain"   "solution"     "α3"            "machine"    
##  [5,] "analysis"    "iot"          "operational"  "complex"       "process"    
##  [6,] "−"           "transmission" "optimization" "receptors"     "2019"       
##  [7,] "land"        "network"      "minimal"      "proteins"      "al"         
##  [8,] "study"       "internet"     "savings"      "receptor"      "development"
##  [9,] "spatial"     "things"       "end"          "binding"       "human"      
## [10,] "urban"       "node"         "amssymb"      "supplementary" "science"    
##       Topic 11     
##  [1,] "models"     
##  [2,] "model"      
##  [3,] "data"       
##  [4,] "learning"   
##  [5,] "prediction" 
##  [6,] "design"     
##  [7,] "methods"    
##  [8,] "performance"
##  [9,] "training"   
## [10,] "accuracy"
theta <- tmResult$topics
beta  <- tmResult$terms
vocab <- colnames(beta)

Step 4: Visualize Top Terms per Topic

The β (φ) matrix gives us P(term | topic). Plotting the highest-probability terms for each topic is the primary tool for human interpretation of what each topic “means.”

topics <- tidy(topicModel_k_select, matrix = "beta")

top_terms <- topics |>
  group_by(topic) |>
  slice_max(beta, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  arrange(topic, -beta)

top_terms |>
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, beta, topic, sep = "")) |>
  ggplot(aes(term, beta, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ topic, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 terms per topic",
       subtitle = "β = P(term | topic)",
       x = NULL, y = "β") +
  theme_minimal()

Step 5: Name the Topics

Interpret each topic by examining its top terms and assigning a descriptive label. This is an inherently qualitative step — topic names should reflect genuine thematic content, not just the top terms mechanically concatenated.

topic_words <- terms(topicModel_k_select, 5)

# Option 1: auto-name from top 5 terms (ok for a first pass)
topic_names <- apply(topic_words, 2, paste, collapse = " ")

# Option 2: replace with your own interpretive labels after inspection
# topic_names <- c("Topic Name 1", "Topic Name 2",
#                  "Topic Name 3",  "Topic Name 4"...)

topic_names
##                                            Topic 1 
##                 "coral reefs climate water global" 
##                                            Topic 2 
##          "ai-2 expression gene bacterial c-di-gmp" 
##                                            Topic 3 
##                    "sleep cells ais cell guidance" 
##                                            Topic 4 
##             "species detection images animal data" 
##                                            Topic 5 
##           "drug model absorption based solubility" 
##                                            Topic 6 
##  "carbon ecosystem development landscape analysis" 
##                                            Topic 7 
##               "data trust security blockchain iot" 
##                                            Topic 8 
## "energy solutions efficiency solution operational" 
##                                            Topic 9 
##                       "protein mm data α3 complex" 
##                                           Topic 10 
##         "data research technology machine process" 
##                                           Topic 11 
##            "models model data learning prediction"

Step 6: Document-Topic Distributions (θ)

The θ matrix shows how each document blends across topics. This reflects the allocation of word tokens z to topics within each document — a document with many words assigned to topic 2 will have high θ for topic 2.

example_ids <- c(1:5)
n <- length(example_ids)

example_props <- theta[example_ids, ]
colnames(example_props) <- topic_names

viz_df <- melt(cbind(data.frame(example_props),
                     document = factor(1:n)),
               variable.name = "topic",
               id.vars = "document")

ggplot(data = viz_df, aes(topic, value, fill = document)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1)) +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ document, ncol = n) +
  labs(title = "Topic distributions across sample documents",
       subtitle = "θ = P(topic | document)",
       x = NULL, y = "Topic proportion")

Step 7: Interactive Visualization with LDAvis

LDAvis provides an interactive view of two complementary aspects of the model:

  • Intertopic distance map: circles represent topics; size = proportion of corpus tokens assigned to that topic; distance = degree of topic overlap in vocabulary
  • Term relevance bar chart: adjustable by the λ (lambda) parameter

The λ (lambda) Relevance Parameter

λ controls the trade-off between term frequency and term exclusivity when ranking words within a topic:

\[\text{Relevance}(w, t) = \lambda \times P(w|t) + (1 - \lambda) \times \frac{P(w|t)}{P(w)}\]

  • λ near 1: ranks terms by raw frequency within the topic — highlights common words
  • λ near 0: ranks terms by how exclusive they are to the topic relative to the whole corpus — highlights distinctive words, analogous to tf-idf
  • λ ≈ 0.6: the empirically recommended default (Sievert & Shirley,
    1. that balances both
svd_tsne <- function(x) tsne(svd(x)$u)

json <- createJSON(
  phi           = tmResult$terms,
  theta         = tmResult$topics,
  doc.length    = rowSums(dfm),
  vocab         = colnames(dfm),
  term.frequency = colSums(dfm),
  mds.method    = svd_tsne,
  plot.opts     = list(xlab = "", ylab = "")
)

serVis(json)

Reading the LDAvis plot: - Circles that are far apart share few words — good topic separation - Overlapping circles suggest those topics may be too similar and k could be reduced - Try λ = 0.6 as a starting point, then adjust to see distinctive vs. frequent terms

Assignment: Lab 4

For this assignment you will apply the full LDA pipeline to the Nexis Uni article data you downloaded in Week 2. Create a new .Rmd and complete the following:

1. Build and clean your corpus
Create a corpus from your articles. Clean appropriately for your data — consider what stopwords, minimum document frequencies, and tokenization choices make sense given your specific corpus.

2. Select k using multiple criteria

Run three models (i.e. with 3 values of k) and select the overall best value for k (the number of topics) - include some justification for your selection: FindTopicsNumber() optimization metrics, theory, interpretability, LDAvis. Select the best single value of k. Criteria to consider:

- Optimization Metrics - Run models across a range of k values and produce a coherence plot using topicdoc.

- Theory: what themes do you expect given your article topic?

- Interpretability: read the top 10 terms at candidate k values — do they form coherent, distinct themes?

- LDAvis: do the topic circles separate well, or do they overlap heavily?

Select a single best k and write 2–3 sentences justifying your choice drawing on the three criteria.

3. Fit your final model and visualize top terms
Plot the top 10 terms per topic.

4. Visualize document-topic distributions
Plot θ for a sample of documents (5–10). Choose a sample size that produces a readable plot.

5. Interpret your topics
Assign a descriptive name to each topic (not just the concatenated top terms). In a short paragraph per topic, describe:

- What theme or issue does this topic represent?

- Which words are most diagnostic of this interpretation?

- Are there any surprising or ambiguous words in the top terms?
